items.q: Does a strong Spearman's rank correlation prove that one variable causes the other?
items.correct: No - correlation shows association only; causation requires theory and further evidence, and a third factor may be driving both variables.
items.misconception: A high correlation coefficient proves causation.
items.random_wrong: Spearman's rank can only be used on temperature data.
items.why: Confusing correlation with causation is the classic statistical error penalised in IA analysis sections.

items.q: Is a bigger sample always better in fieldwork?
items.correct: A larger sample reduces random error, but the sampling strategy must also be unbiased and practical; a big biased sample is still misleading.
items.misconception: Sample size is the only thing that matters when collecting data.
items.random_wrong: A sample of one site is fine if it is measured very carefully.
items.why: Students chase quantity over design; random, systematic or stratified strategy matters as much as the number of measurements.

items.q: If your data do not support your hypothesis, has the fieldwork failed?
items.correct: No - rejecting a hypothesis is a valid scientific outcome; the report should evaluate why, considering methods, anomalies and the underlying theory.
items.misconception: Fieldwork only succeeds if the hypothesis is confirmed.
items.random_wrong: You should adjust the data so the hypothesis is supported.
items.why: The IA rewards critical evaluation; an unexpected result analysed honestly can score higher than a tidy confirmation.

items.q: Which statistical test compares observed with expected frequencies?
items.why: It's Chi-squared. “Spearman's rank” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Spearman's rank is used to test what?
items.correct: The strength of correlation between two ranked variables
items.why: It's The strength of correlation between two ranked variables. “The difference between two maps” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What should a good fieldwork hypothesis be?
items.correct: A clear, testable statement linking two measurable variables
items.misconception: A vague question with no variables
items.why: It's A clear, testable statement linking two measurable variables. “A vague question with no variables” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which is an ethical consideration in geography fieldwork?
items.correct: Gaining consent and avoiding harm to people and environments
items.misconception: Using the largest sample regardless of disturbance caused
items.random_wrong: Keeping your results secret from your teacher
items.why: Correct answer: Gaining consent and avoiding harm to people and environments. “Using the largest sample regardless of disturbance caused” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Common significance level for hypothesis testing?
items.why: Exact answer: 0.05 p. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
